claim: Only 32% of trial signups in a 6-person design agency create tasks in a shared location. Tasks assigned via Slack DMs get buried. Two designers worked on the same icon set (8 hours wasted) because assignments were made in separate conversations 3 days apart.
source: User interview 01 — Maria, team lead at 6-person design agency

claim: Each team member knows only about 70% of what everyone else is working on. The 30% visibility gap causes problems at least twice a week: duplicate work and tasks nobody started because everyone assumed someone else would.
source: User interview 03 — Priya, co-founder of 4-person startup

claim: Survey (N=127): 53% of respondents in teams of 2-8 people lose or forget tasks at least once per week. 38% experience duplicate work multiple times per month. Average respondent uses 2.3 tracking methods simultaneously.
source: Online survey — small team task management (N=127)
methodology_note: Self-selected sample via indie hacker/freelancer communities. ±8.7% margin at 95% confidence.

claim: Survey demographics: team leads (42%), individual contributors (34%), founders (24%). Industries: tech (31%), creative/design (22%), consulting (18%), marketing (14%). Team sizes: 2-3 (38%), 4-5 (35%), 6-8 (27%).

Freelance developer spends ~20 minutes every morning sending WhatsApp messages to subcontractors asking for status updates. A missed dependency (copywriter didn't know designer needed FAQ copy first) caused a client-facing deadline miss. Described client relationship damage as the highest-impact consequence.
source: User interview 02 — James, freelance developer managing 2-3 subcontractors

claim: Observed during interviews: Maria took 90 seconds to find a task in Slack. James took over 2 minutes to find a task update in WhatsApp. Priya spent 3 minutes trying to determine if a task was complete. Task assignment via chat takes 2-5 minutes including context-switching.
Survey: median daily time spent on manual task coordination is approximately 20 minutes. 48% spend 20+ minutes per day. 34% spend 10-20 minutes. Only 18% spend less than 10 minutes.

53% of survey respondents (67 of 127) have tried and abandoned a dedicated task management tool. Top reasons: too complex for team size (58%), felt like overhead not help (44%), team didn't adopt consistently (49%), free tier too limited (31%), onboarding took too long (27%).
source: Online survey — tool abandonment section (N=67 who tried and stopped)

claim: Survey open-ended responses (coded): simplicity/minimal features requested by 56%, shared visibility by 50%, fast task creation (<30 sec) by 38%, no account/login requirement by 24%, works on mobile web by 22%, free by 21%.
source: Online survey — open-ended responses, coded by 2 researchers (89% inter-rater agreement)

claim: Priya's Google Sheet 'Master Task List' had 23 rows: 8 with blank status, 5 with overdue due dates still marked 'In Progress'. Last updated 3 weeks prior to interview. Sheet abandoned because it requires deliberate effort outside natural workflow.
source: User interview 03 — Priya, behavioral observation during interview

claim: Survey statistical analysis: teams of 4-5 people report the highest rate of weekly forgotten tasks (61%), compared to 2-3 (44%) and 6-8 (52%). Correlation: teams using 3+ tracking methods simultaneously report higher task loss frequency (r=0.42, p<0.01).

Competitor analysis: no existing tool (Trello, Todoist, Asana, Linear, Google Sheets) is designed specifically for teams of 2-8 people who need shared task visibility with near-zero setup. Trello/Asana are too complex, Todoist is too personal, Linear is engineering-specific, Sheets/Notion DIY is too fragile.
